items.label: "subarray", "contiguous", "window"
items.code: → Sliding Window / Prefix Sum

items.label: "substring" with condition
items.code: → Sliding Window + HashMap

items.label: "top k", "k most frequent", "kth largest"
items.code: → Heap / Bucket Sort

items.label: "all combinations", "all permutations", "generate all"
items.code: → Backtracking

items.label: "shortest path", "minimum steps", "fewest moves"
items.code: → BFS (unweighted) / Dijkstra (weighted)

items.label: "number of islands", "connected", "regions"
items.code: → DFS / BFS / Union-Find

items.label: "parentheses", "brackets", "valid nesting"
items.code: → Stack

items.label: "next greater", "next smaller", "stock span"
items.code: → Monotonic Stack

items.label: "palindrome"
items.code: → Two Pointers (from center or edges) / DP

items.label: "anagram", "permutation of"
items.code: → Counter / Sorted Comparison

items.label: "duplicate", "contains", "seen before"
items.code: → Set / HashMap

items.label: "sorted array" + search
items.code: → Binary Search

items.label: "minimum cost", "maximum profit", "number of ways"
items.code: → DP (or Greedy)

items.label: "merge intervals", "overlapping"
items.code: → Sort by Start + Scan

items.label: "in-place", "O(1) space"
items.code: → Two Pointers / Swap Trick

items.label: n ≤ 10-20
items.code: → O(2^n) or O(n!)  →  Backtracking, Bitmask DP
items.note: Small n = brute force is fine

items.label: n ≤ 100-500
items.code: → O(n²) or O(n³)  →  DP (2D), Nested loops

items.label: n ≤ 10⁴-10⁵
items.code: → O(n log n)  →  Sort, Heap, Binary Search, Divide & Conquer

items.label: n ≤ 10⁵-10⁶
items.code: → O(n)  →  HashMap, Sliding Window, Prefix Sum, Bucket Sort

items.label: n ≤ 10⁹+
items.code: → O(log n) or O(1)  →  Binary Search, Math, Bit Manipulation

items.label: Array of numbers
items.code: → Two Pointers, Sliding Window, Prefix Sum, Sort
items.note: Ask: is it sorted? Does order matter?

items.label: String
items.code: → Sliding Window, Stack, DP, Two Pointers
items.note: Immutable — think about building with list + join

items.label: Matrix / Grid
items.code: → BFS/DFS (flood fill), DP (paths)

items.label: Tree
items.code: → DFS (recursive), BFS (level order)
items.note: Most tree problems are DFS with return values

items.label: Graph (edge list)
items.code: → Build adjacency list → BFS/DFS/Topological Sort

items.label: Sorted input
items.code: → Binary Search, Two Pointers

items.label: Unsorted but need order
items.code: → Sort first, then Scan / Two Pointers / Greedy
items.note: Sorting is O(n log n) — often worth it

items.label: Linked List
items.code: → Two Pointers (slow/fast), Dummy Head

items.label: Top K problems
items.code:
Bounded range (count ≤ n)?     → Bucket sort    O(n)
Need streaming / online?       → Heap           O(n log k)
One-shot, average case OK?     → Quickselect    O(n) avg
Just need answer, not optimal? → Sort           O(n log n)

items.label: Existence / lookup problems
items.code:
Just yes/no lookup?            → Set            O(1)
Need associated value?         → HashMap        O(1)
Already sorted?                → Binary Search  O(log n)
Need to check range/window?    → Sorted + bisect

items.label: Subarray with constraint
items.code:
Contiguous, shrinkable window? → Sliding Window   O(n)
Sum equals target?             → Prefix Sum + Map O(n)
Fixed size window?             → Sliding Window    O(n)
Need min/max in window?        → Monotonic Deque   O(n)

items.label: Ordering / next element
items.code:
Next greater/smaller element?  → Monotonic Stack  O(n)
Need relative ordering?        → Sort             O(n log n)
Nested intervals/parens?       → Stack            O(n)

items.label: Graph traversal
items.code:
Shortest path, unweighted?     → BFS             O(V+E)
Explore/count components?      → DFS or UF       O(V+E)
Shortest path, weighted?       → Dijkstra (heap) O(E log V)
Dynamic connectivity?          → Union-Find      O(α(n))

items.label: Optimization problems
items.code:
Optimal substructure + overlap? → DP
Locally optimal = globally?     → Greedy
Need all combinations?          → Backtracking
Yes/no feasibility + monotonic? → Binary Search on Answer

items.label: 1. What input?
items.code: array / string / tree / graph / matrix / linked list
items.note: Input shape narrows your options immediately

items.label: 2. What keywords?
items.code:
subarray → window    shortest → BFS
all → backtrack      sorted → binary search
top k → heap         duplicate → set/map
items.note: Keywords in the problem map to patterns

items.label: 3. What constraints?
items.code:
n size → allowed time complexity
n ≤ 20: brute force    n ≤ 10⁵: O(n log n) or O(n)
n ≤ 500: O(n²)         n ≤ 10⁹: O(log n)
items.note: Constraints tell you WHICH approach to pick

items.label: 4. Does sorting help?
items.code:
If order doesn't matter → sort first
Sorting unlocks: two pointers, binary search, greedy
items.note: Sorting is O(n log n) — often the key insight

items.label: 5. Do I need to remember something?
items.code:
Previously seen values → HashMap / Set
Previous computed results → DP table
Previous states → Stack / Queue
items.note: If yes → pick the right memory structure

items.label: Converging Pointers
Same-Direction (Partitioning)
items.code:
def remove_duplicates(nums):
    slow = 0                          # write pointer
    for fast in range(len(nums)):     # read pointer
        if nums[fast] != nums[slow]:
            slow += 1
            nums[slow] = nums[fast]
    return slow + 1

items.label: Fast / Slow (Cycle Detection)
Pointer Initialization
items.code:
Converging:   left=0, right=n-1   (sorted input, pair search)
Same-dir:     slow=0, fast=0       (partition, remove dupes)
Fast/slow:    both at head         (cycle detect, find middle)
items.note: Converging requires sorted input or search from both ends

items.label: Loop Condition
items.code:
left < right       → converging, don't want pointers to cross
left <= right      → when single-element answer is valid
fast < n           → same-direction scan
fast and fast.next → linked list cycle detection
items.note: Off-by-one: < vs <= changes whether you process the meeting point

items.label: Move Criteria
items.code:
Converging: move the pointer that gets closer to target
Same-dir:   fast always moves; slow moves on condition
Fast/slow:  slow moves 1, fast moves 2 every step
items.note: For 3Sum: fix one, two-pointer on rest → O(n²)

items.label: Computation
items.code:
Track: current sum, window contents, write position
Update answer when condition is met
For partitioning: swap or overwrite at slow pointer

items.label: 3Sum / kSum
items.code:
Sort + fix outer elements + two-pointer inner
Skip duplicates: while l < r and nums[l] == nums[l-1]: l += 1

items.label: Container With Most Water
items.code:
Move the shorter side inward (greedy)
area = min(h[l], h[r]) * (r - l)

items.label: Trapping Rain Water
items.code:
Two pointers + track left_max, right_max
Process the smaller side: water = max_height - curr_height

items.label: Essential Problems
items.code:
167 Two Sum II (converging)
 15 3Sum (sort + converging)
 11 Container With Most Water (greedy converging)
 26 Remove Duplicates (same-direction)
283 Move Zeroes (same-direction swap)
141 Linked List Cycle (fast/slow)
 42 Trapping Rain Water (two-pointer + tracking)

items.label: Variable-Size Window
items.code:
def sliding_window(s):
    left = 0
    window = {}          # or set, counter, running sum
    result = 0
    for right in range(len(s)):
        # EXPAND: add s[right] to window
        window[s[right]] = window.get(s[right], 0) + 1

        # SHRINK: while window is invalid
        while WINDOW_INVALID:
            # remove s[left] from window
            window[s[left]] -= 1
            if window[s[left]] == 0:
                del window[s[left]]
            left += 1

        # UPDATE: result with current valid window
        result = max(result, right - left + 1)
    return result

items.label: Fixed-Size Window
items.code:
def fixed_window(nums, k):
    window_sum = sum(nums[:k])
    result = window_sum
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        window_sum += nums[i] - nums[i - k]   # slide
        result = max(result, window_sum)
    return result

items.label: Window State
items.code:
HashMap/Counter → character frequencies (substring problems)
Running sum     → subarray sum problems
Set             → unique element tracking
Deque           → sliding window min/max
items.note: Choose based on what constraint you're tracking

items.label: Shrink Condition
items.code:
len(window) > k          → too many distinct chars
window_sum > target      → sum exceeds target
not all_chars_covered    → haven't met requirement yet (expand more)
items.note: Minimum window → shrink when valid; Maximum window → shrink when invalid

items.label: When to Update Result
items.code:
Maximum window: update AFTER shrinking (window is valid)
Minimum window: update WHILE shrinking (each valid state)
Fixed window:   update every step after reaching size k

items.label: Minimum Window Substring
items.code:
Track need (Counter of t) and have (count of satisfied chars)
Shrink while have == len(need) → update min each time

items.label: Longest Substring Without Repeats
items.code: Set-based window; shrink while s[right] in window

items.label: Subarray Sum Equals K (not sliding window!)
items.code:
Use prefix sum + HashMap instead
Can't shrink window when sum can decrease (negative numbers)
items.note: Problem: 560 — common trap: this is NOT a sliding window problem

items.label: Essential Problems
items.code:
3 Longest Substring Without Repeating (variable)
 76 Minimum Window Substring (variable, min)
209 Minimum Size Subarray Sum (variable, min)
424 Longest Repeating Character Replacement (variable)
567 Permutation in String (fixed concept)
239 Sliding Window Maximum (fixed + deque)

items.label: Exact Match
Left Bound (bisect_left)
Binary Search on Answer
Loop Condition
items.code:
lo <= hi  → exact match (search space shrinks to 0)
lo < hi   → boundary search (converge to 1 element)
lo < hi-1 → when you need to check both lo and hi after
items.note: Most common mistake: wrong loop condition → infinite loop or off-by-one

items.label: Boundaries
items.code:
Exact:       lo=0, hi=n-1, while lo<=hi
Left bound:  lo=0, hi=n,   while lo<hi
Right bound: lo=0, hi=n,   while lo<hi  (flip condition)
On answer:   lo=min_possible, hi=max_possible
items.note: hi=n (not n-1) for boundary searches to handle 'not found'

items.label: Midpoint Update
items.code:
Found exact:     return mid
Go right:        lo = mid + 1
Go left:         hi = mid - 1  (exact) or hi = mid (boundary)
NEVER: lo = mid with lo < hi → infinite loop
items.note: For right bound: lo = mid + 1 when nums[mid] <= target

items.label: Rotated Sorted Array
items.code:
Find which half is sorted, check if target is in that half
if nums[lo] <= nums[mid]: left half sorted
else: right half sorted

items.label: Search on Answer
items.code:
Min: lo=min, hi=max → shrink hi when feasible
Max: lo=min, hi=max → shrink lo when feasible
Key: define feasible() and monotonicity

items.label: 2D Matrix Search
items.code:
Treat m×n matrix as sorted array of length m*n
row = mid // n, col = mid % n

items.label: Essential Problems
items.code:
33 Search in Rotated Sorted Array
 34 Find First and Last Position
153 Find Minimum in Rotated Array
875 Koko Eating Bananas (search on answer)
410 Split Array Largest Sum (search on answer)
  4 Median of Two Sorted Arrays (hard)

items.label: Basic BFS (Graph)
Level-Order BFS
Grid BFS (Shortest Path)
When to Use BFS vs DFS
items.code:
BFS: shortest path (unweighted), level-order, nearest X
DFS: explore all paths, connected components, cycle detect
BFS guarantees shortest path; DFS does NOT
items.note: BFS = breadth first = queue; DFS = depth first = stack/recursion

items.label: When to Track Levels
items.code:
Need distance/depth?         → track level with for-loop
Just reachability?           → basic BFS (no level tracking)
Multi-source (rotten oranges) → start with ALL sources in queue
items.note: Level = distance from source in unweighted graph

items.label: Visited Set Timing
items.code:
Add to visited WHEN ENQUEUING (not when processing)
This prevents duplicate entries in queue
For grid: visited.add((nr,nc)) BEFORE queue.append()
items.note: Common bug: adding to visited when popping → TLE from duplicates

items.label: Multi-Source BFS
items.code:
Start with ALL sources in queue at once
queue = deque(all_sources)
visited = set(all_sources)
# Process normally — level = distance from nearest source

items.label: 0-1 BFS
items.code:
Edges with weight 0 or 1: use deque
Weight 0 → appendleft; Weight 1 → append
Achieves Dijkstra-like result in O(V+E)

items.label: Topological Sort (BFS / Kahn's)
items.code:
Start with all nodes with in-degree 0
Process → reduce neighbors' in-degree
If neighbor in-degree becomes 0 → enqueue

items.label: Essential Problems
items.code:
200 Number of Islands (grid BFS/DFS)
994 Rotting Oranges (multi-source BFS)
127 Word Ladder (shortest path BFS)
207 Course Schedule (topological BFS)
102 Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
743 Network Delay Time (Dijkstra for weighted)

items.label: Recursive DFS (Graph)
Iterative DFS (Stack)
Grid DFS (Flood Fill)
DFS with Return Value (Trees)
Recursive vs Iterative
items.code:
Recursive: cleaner, natural for trees, uses call stack
Iterative: avoids stack overflow for deep graphs
Python recursion limit: ~1000 (sys.setrecursionlimit(N))
items.note: Use iterative for very deep graphs (n > 1000)

items.label: Visited Strategy
items.code:
Global visited set → explore all reachable nodes
Path visited (add/remove) → detect cycles in directed graph
Modify grid in-place → mark visited by changing cell value

items.label: Return Value Design
items.code:
Count components → return nothing, count dfs() calls
Max depth/diameter → return depth, track answer globally
Path exists → return True/False
Collect results → append to shared list

items.label: Connected Components
Cycle Detection (Directed)
items.code:
Use 3 colors: WHITE (unvisited), GRAY (in current path), BLACK (done)
Cycle exists if we visit a GRAY node

items.label: Tree Diameter
items.code:
Track max diameter globally
At each node: diameter = left_depth + right_depth
Return 1 + max(left, right) as depth

items.label: Essential Problems
items.code:
200 Number of Islands (grid flood fill)
695 Max Area of Island (grid DFS + return size)
133 Clone Graph (DFS + HashMap)
104 Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
543 Diameter of Binary Tree (DFS return value)
417 Pacific Atlantic Water Flow (multi-source DFS)

items.label: General Backtracking
items.code:
def backtrack(result, path, choices):
    if IS_COMPLETE(path):          # base case
        result.append(path[:])     # copy path!
        return
    for choice in choices:
        if not is_valid(choice):   # pruning
            continue
        path.append(choice)        # choose
        backtrack(result, path, NEXT_CHOICES)
        path.pop()                 # un-choose (backtrack)

items.label: Subsets
Permutations
Subsets vs Permutations vs Combos
items.code:
Subsets:       start index, no length requirement
Combinations:  start index + target length or sum
Permutations:  used[] array, all elements used
Key: subsets/combos use start index; perms use used[]
items.note: Start index prevents [1,2] and [2,1] from both appearing

items.label: When to Copy Path
items.code:
result.append(path[:])   # MUST copy!
result.append(list(path)) # also works
# WRONG: result.append(path) → all entries point to same list
items.note: path is mutated during backtracking, so you must snapshot it

items.label: Handling Duplicates
items.code:
Sort input first, then skip duplicates:
if i > start and nums[i] == nums[i-1]: continue
For permutations:
if i > 0 and nums[i] == nums[i-1] and not used[i-1]: continue
items.note: This is critical for problems like 40 (Combination Sum II), 47 (Permutations II)

items.label: Combination Sum (reuse allowed)
items.code:
backtrack(i, path)  instead of backtrack(i+1, path)
Allows same element to be chosen again

items.label: N-Queens / Sudoku
items.code:
Validate placement before recursing
Track columns, diagonals in sets for O(1) check

items.label: Word Search (grid backtracking)
items.code:
Mark cell as visited → explore 4 directions → unmark
Combines grid DFS with backtracking

items.label: Essential Problems
items.code:
78 Subsets
 46 Permutations
 39 Combination Sum (reuse)
 40 Combination Sum II (no reuse + dedup)
 17 Letter Combinations of Phone Number
 79 Word Search (grid backtracking)

items.label: 1D DP (Bottom-Up)
items.code:
def solve(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    dp = [0] * (n + 1)          # dp[i] = answer for first i items
    dp[0] = BASE_CASE
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        dp[i] = RECURRENCE(dp, i, nums)
    return dp[n]

items.label: 1D DP — Climbing Stairs Pattern
2D DP
Top-Down (Memoization)
Identify the State
items.code:
What changes between subproblems?
Index i              → 1D DP
Two indices (i, j)   → 2D DP
Index + extra flag   → state machine DP
Bitmask of used items → bitmask DP (n ≤ 20)
items.note: State = minimum info needed to make future decisions

items.label: Write the Recurrence
items.code:
dp[i] = max/min of:
  - dp[i-1] + something   (take current)
  - dp[i-1]               (skip current)
  - dp[i-k] + ...         (jump from earlier state)

Common: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i])  (House Robber)
items.note: Start with brute-force recursion, then memoize

items.label: Bottom-Up vs Top-Down
items.code:
Top-down:  easier to write, only computes needed states
Bottom-up: faster (no recursion overhead), easier to optimize space
Space optimization: if dp[i] only depends on dp[i-1], use 2 vars
items.note: Top-down for complex state spaces; bottom-up for simple 1D/2D

items.label: Knapsack Pattern
items.code:
dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-wt[i]] + val[i])
0/1 knapsack: each item used once
Unbounded: dp[i][w-wt[i]] (same row)

items.label: State Machine DP
items.code:
Best Time to Buy/Sell Stock:
hold[i] = max(hold[i-1], rest[i-1] - price)
rest[i] = max(rest[i-1], hold[i-1] + price)

items.label: Interval DP
Essential Problems
items.code:

198 House Robber (1D skip/take)
322 Coin Change (1D unbounded)
300 Longest Increasing Subsequence (1D)
1143 Longest Common Subsequence (2D)

416 Partition Equal Subset Sum (0/1 knapsack)

items.label: Next Greater Element
Previous Smaller Element
Monotonic Direction
items.code:
Decreasing stack → finds NEXT GREATER  (pop when curr > top)
Increasing stack → finds NEXT SMALLER  (pop when curr < top)
Stack stores: indices (usually) or values
items.note: Decreasing = each new element ≤ top; pop when this breaks

items.label: What Happens on Pop
items.code:
Popped element found its answer:
  Next greater: answer = current element
  Next smaller: answer = current element
  Histogram area: width = i - stack[-1] - 1 (or i if stack empty)
items.note: The 'pop' is where the magic happens — that's when you compute

items.label: Circular Array
items.code:
Iterate 2*n, use i % n for index
Handles wrap-around (e.g., Next Greater Element II)

items.label: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
items.code:
For each bar, find left and right boundaries
Area = height[i] * (right_bound - left_bound - 1)
Use monotonic increasing stack
items.note: Problem: 84 — extend to 85 (Maximal Rectangle) with row-by-row histograms

items.label: Daily Temperatures
items.code: result[idx] = i - idx (distance, not value)

items.label: Stock Span
items.code:
Count consecutive days with price ≤ today
Monotonic decreasing stack of indices

items.label: Essential Problems
items.code:
739 Daily Temperatures (next warmer day)
496 Next Greater Element I
 84 Largest Rectangle in Histogram
 85 Maximal Rectangle (histogram extension)
503 Next Greater Element II (circular)
901 Online Stock Span

items.label: Basic Prefix Sum
Subarray Sum Equals K
items.code:
def subarray_sum(nums, k):
    count = 0
    curr_sum = 0
    prefix_counts = {0: 1}            # empty prefix
    for x in nums:
        curr_sum += x
        # if curr_sum - k exists in prefix, we found a subarray
        count += prefix_counts.get(curr_sum - k, 0)
        prefix_counts[curr_sum] = prefix_counts.get(curr_sum, 0) + 1
    return count

items.label: 2D Prefix Sum
When to Use
items.code:
Multiple range sum queries      → build prefix once, O(1) per query
Count subarrays with sum = k   → prefix sum + HashMap
Subarray sum with negative nums → prefix sum (NOT sliding window!)
items.note: Sliding window only works when all values are positive

items.label: HashMap Pattern
items.code:
Key insight: sum(nums[i:j]) = prefix[j] - prefix[i]
So subarray sum = k ⟺ prefix[j] - prefix[i] = k
⟺ prefix[i] = prefix[j] - k
Store prefix sum frequencies in HashMap
items.note: Initialize: {0: 1} to handle subarrays starting at index 0

items.label: Prefix Sum vs Sliding Window
items.code:
All positive + contiguous window → Sliding Window
Negative numbers + sum condition → Prefix Sum + Map
Fixed-size window sum           → either works
Range queries on static array   → Prefix Sum

items.label: Running Sum / Cumulative
items.code:
prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + nums[i]
Direct modification of array as prefix sum

items.label: Subarray Divisible by K
items.code:
Same HashMap pattern but track curr_sum % k
Two prefixes with same remainder → their difference is divisible by k

items.label: Product Except Self
items.code:
Left prefix product + Right prefix product
result[i] = left_product[i] * right_product[i]

items.label: Essential Problems
items.code:
560 Subarray Sum Equals K (prefix + HashMap)
238 Product of Array Except Self (prefix product)
303 Range Sum Query - Immutable (basic prefix)
304 Range Sum Query 2D (2D prefix)
974 Subarray Sums Divisible by K
523 Continuous Subarray Sum

items.label: Merge Intervals
Insert Interval
items.code:
def insert(intervals, new):
    result = []
    for i, (s, e) in enumerate(intervals):
        if new[1] < s:              # new ends before current
            result.append(new)
            return result + intervals[i:]
        elif new[0] > e:            # new starts after current
            result.append([s, e])
        else:                       # overlap → merge
            new = [min(new[0], s), max(new[1], e)]
    result.append(new)
    return result

items.label: Interval Overlap Check
Sort Strategy
items.code:
Merge intervals    → sort by start
Remove min overlaps → sort by end (greedy: keep earliest-ending)
Meeting rooms       → sort starts and ends separately, or sort by start
items.note: Sort order determines the greedy strategy

items.label: Overlap Detection
items.code:
Two intervals overlap: a.start < b.end AND b.start < a.end
Adjacent (touching):   a.end == b.start (may or may not count)
Contained:             a.start <= b.start AND b.end <= a.end

items.label: When to Use Sweep Line
items.code:
Count max overlapping at any point → sweep line
Create events: +1 at start, -1 at end
Sort events, scan with running count → max = answer

items.label: Meeting Rooms II (Min Rooms)
items.code:
Sort by start; use min-heap of end times
If curr.start >= heap[0]: pop (room freed)
Always push curr.end; answer = max heap size

items.label: Non-Overlapping Intervals
items.code:
Sort by end time (greedy)
Keep track of last end; count removals when overlap
items.note: Problem: 435 — sort by END is key (not start)

items.label: Sweep Line (Max Concurrent)
items.code:
events = []
for s, e in intervals:
    events.append((s, 1))    # start
    events.append((e, -1))   # end
events.sort()
curr = max_concurrent = 0
for _, delta in events:
    curr += delta
    max_concurrent = max(max_concurrent, curr)
items.note: Useful when you need max/min overlap count

items.label: Essential Problems
items.code:
56 Merge Intervals
 57 Insert Interval
435 Non-overlapping Intervals (greedy by end)
252 Meeting Rooms (sort + check)
253 Meeting Rooms II (heap or sweep line)
986 Interval List Intersections

items.label: Empty list
items.code: lst = []

items.label: Repeated value
items.code: lst = [0] * n
items.note: Careful: [[]] * n creates n refs to SAME inner list

items.label: From range
items.code: lst = list(range(10))

items.label: From string
items.code: list('abc')  # ['a', 'b', 'c']

items.label: 2D matrix
items.code: grid = [[0] * cols for _ in range(rows)]

items.label: append(x)
items.code: lst.append(x)

items.label: pop()
items.code:
lst.pop()  # last
lst.pop(i)  # at index
items.note: pop() is O(1), pop(i) is O(n)

items.label: insert(i, x)
items.code: lst.insert(0, x)  # prepend

items.label: extend

l, r = 0, len(nums) - 1
while l < r:

    elif nums[l] + nums[r] < target: l += 1
    else: r -= 1

items.label: Sliding window

# sum(nums[i:j]) = prefix[j] - prefix[i]

items.label: Visited tracking
Longest consecutive
items.code:
s = set(nums)
for x in s:
    if x - 1 not in s:  # start of sequence
        length = 0
        while x + length in s:
            length += 1
        best = max(best, length)

items.label: Use a list as stack
items.code: stack = []
items.note: Python has no built-in stack class — list works perfectly

items.label: Initialize with values
items.code: stack = [1, 2, 3]  # 3 is top

items.label: push
items.code: stack.append(x)

items.label: pop
items.code: stack.pop()

items.label: peek
items.code: stack[-1]

items.label: is empty
items.code:
if not stack:  # Pythonic
if len(stack) == 0:  # also works

items.label: size
items.code: len(stack)

items.label: Check before pop
items.code:
# WRONG: stack.pop()  — may raise IndexError
# RIGHT: if stack: stack.pop()
# Or: val = stack.pop() if stack else default

items.label: Don't use pop(0)
items.code:
# stack.pop(0) is O(n) — that's a queue operation
# For FIFO, use collections.deque

items.label: Valid parentheses
Monotonic stack
items.code:
# Next greater element
result = [-1] * len(nums)
stack = []  # indices
for i, x in enumerate(nums):
    while stack and nums[stack[-1]] < x:
        result[stack.pop()] = x
    stack.append(i)

items.note: This is why deque > list for queues

q[0]    # front

q.rotate(1)   # shift right
q.rotate(-1)  # shift left

items.label: index access
items.code: q[i]  # works but O(n)!
items.note: Deque is NOT good for random access

items.label: Don't use list as queue
items.code:
# list.pop(0) is O(n)! Shifts all elements.
# Always use deque for FIFO queues.

items.label: No slicing

# q[1:3] → TypeError

BFS with levels
Sliding window max
Import
items.code: import heapq

items.label: From list (heapify)
items.code:
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
heapq.heapify(nums)  # in-place, O(n)

items.label: Empty heap
items.code: heap = []  # just a list

items.label: Max heap trick
items.code:
# Python only has min-heap
# Negate values for max-heap:
heapq.heappush(heap, -val)
max_val = -heapq.heappop(heap)

items.label: push
items.code: heapq.heappush(heap, x)

items.label: pop (smallest)
items.code: heapq.heappop(heap)

items.label: peek
items.code: heap[0]  # smallest element

items.label: push + pop
items.code:
heapq.heappushpop(heap, x)  # push then pop
heapq.heapreplace(heap, x)  # pop then push

items.label: n largest/smallest
items.code:
heapq.nlargest(k, iterable)
heapq.nsmallest(k, iterable)

items.label: Tuple ordering
items.code:
heapq.heappush(heap, (priority, item))
# Sorts by first element, then second, etc.
items.note: Use counter as tiebreaker if items aren't comparable

items.label: Min-heap only
items.code:
# Python heapq is always a min-heap
# For max-heap: negate values or use tuple trick

items.label: No decrease-key
items.code:
# heapq has no decrease-key / remove operation
# Workaround: lazy deletion — mark entries as invalid
# and skip them when popping

items.label: Tuple comparison
items.code:
# If priorities are equal, Python compares next element
# If items aren't comparable → TypeError
# Fix: use counter as tiebreaker
count = 0
heapq.heappush(heap, (priority, count, item))
count += 1

items.label: Top K frequent
items.code:
from collections import Counter
import heapq
counts = Counter(nums)
return heapq.nlargest(k, counts, key=counts.get)

items.label: Merge K sorted lists
Median finder (two heaps)
items.code:
small = []  # max-heap (negated)
large = []  # min-heap
def addNum(num):
    heapq.heappush(small, -num)
    heapq.heappush(large, -heapq.heappop(small))
    if len(large) > len(small):
        heapq.heappush(small, -heapq.heappop(large))

# WRONG (O(n²)):
result = ''
for c in chars: result += c

# RIGHT (O(n)):
result = ''.join(chars)
items.note: Each += creates a new string object

'10' < '9'  # True! (compares char by char)

result = ''.join(chars)

s == s[::-1]

while l < r:

    l += 1; r -= 1

Counter(s1) == Counter(s2)

items.label: Binary search
items.code:
l, r = 0, len(nums) - 1
while l <= r:
    mid = (l + r) // 2
    if nums[mid] == target: return mid
    elif nums[mid] < target: l = mid + 1
    else: r = mid - 1

break / continue / else

items.label: if/else position

# Filter (after for):  [x for x in lst if x > 0]
# Transform (before for): [x if x > 0 else 0 for x in lst]
# These are different syntax!

items.label: Readability

# Use regular loops for complex logic

items.label: Side effects

# RIGHT: for x in lst: print(x)

items.label: sorted() — new list
items.code:
sorted(lst)            # ascending
sorted(lst, reverse=True)  # descending

items.label: list.sort() — in-place
items.code:

lst.sort(reverse=True) # descending

items.label: Key function

sorted(lst, key=str.lower)      # case-insensitive

items.label: Multiple criteria

sorted(people, key=lambda x: (x.age, x.name))
items.note: Tuple comparison goes left to right

items.label: Negate for reverse
items.code:
# Sort by score DESC, name ASC
sorted(lst, key=lambda x: (-x[1], x[0]))

items.label: Custom comparator
Stability
items.code:
# Python sort is stable — equal elements keep original order
# This allows multi-pass sorting:
lst.sort(key=lambda x: x.name)   # secondary
lst.sort(key=lambda x: x.age)    # primary

items.label: bisect (binary search)
items.code:
import bisect
bisect.bisect_left(sorted_lst, x)   # leftmost insertion point
bisect.bisect_right(sorted_lst, x)  # rightmost insertion point
bisect.insort(sorted_lst, x)        # insert maintaining order

items.label: sort() returns None

# WRONG: lst = lst.sort()
# RIGHT: lst.sort()  (in-place)

# sorted([1, 'a']) → TypeError in Python 3
# All elements must be comparable

# Can't negate strings for reverse sort
# Use cmp_to_key or multiple stable sorts instead

items.label: Interval merging
Custom sort (Largest Number)
items.code:
from functools import cmp_to_key
def compare(a, b):
    if a + b > b + a: return -1
    elif a + b < b + a: return 1
    return 0
nums_str = sorted(map(str, nums), key=cmp_to_key(compare))

items.label: Bucket sort

# When range is known and small
buckets = [0] * (max_val + 1)
for x in nums:
    buckets[x] += 1

items.label: Sort + two pointers
items.code:
nums.sort()
l, r = 0, len(nums) - 1
while l < r:
    # process sorted pairs

items.label: Mutable default args
items.code:
# WRONG:
def f(lst=[]):
    lst.append(1)  # shared across calls!
    return lst

# RIGHT:
def f(lst=None):
    if lst is None: lst = []
    lst.append(1)
    return lst

items.label: is vs ==
items.code:
a = [1, 2]; b = [1, 2]
a == b  # True (same value)
a is b  # False (different objects)
# Use 'is' only for None, True, False

items.label: Integer caching
items.code:
a = 256; b = 256; a is b  # True (cached)
a = 257; b = 257; a is b  # False (not cached)
# Always use == for value comparison

items.label: Copy vs reference
items.code:
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a        # same object!
b.append(4)  # a is also [1,2,3,4]

b = a[:]     # shallow copy
b = a.copy() # shallow copy
import copy
b = copy.deepcopy(a)  # deep copy

items.label: Integer division
items.code:
7 / 2    # 3.5 (float division)
7 // 2   # 3   (floor division)
-7 // 2  # -4  (rounds toward -inf, NOT toward 0!)

items.label: Infinity
items.code:
float('inf')   # positive infinity
float('-inf')  # negative infinity
# Useful for initializing min/max

items.label: Modulo with negatives
items.code:
-7 % 3   # 2 in Python (always non-negative)
# In C/Java: -7 % 3 = -1
# Python: result has same sign as divisor

items.label: Large numbers
items.code:
# Python ints have arbitrary precision
# No overflow! But be aware of time complexity
2 ** 1000  # works fine

items.label: Off-by-one errors
items.code:
range(n)       # 0 to n-1
range(1, n+1)  # 1 to n
lst[l:r]       # includes l, excludes r
# Binary search: l <= r vs l < r depends on variant

items.label: Forgetting return
items.code:
# Python functions return None by default
# In recursion, forgetting return is a common bug:
def search(node):
    if node.val == target: return node
    # WRONG: search(node.left)
    # RIGHT: return search(node.left)

items.label: Global vs local
items.code:
# In nested functions:
count = 0
def helper():
    nonlocal count  # required to modify outer variable
    count += 1

# For mutable objects, no nonlocal needed:
result = []
def helper():
    result.append(1)  # modifying, not reassigning

items.label: None checks
items.code:
# WRONG: if node == None
# RIGHT: if node is None
# Also: 0, '', [], {}, set() are all falsy
# if not lst: handles empty list AND None

items.label: any / all
items.code:
any(x > 5 for x in nums)  # True if any match
all(x > 0 for x in nums)  # True if all match

items.label: min / max with key
items.code:
min(words, key=len)
max(d, key=d.get)  # key with highest value

items.label: Walrus operator (3.8+)
